Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JA, Steven Chong JA
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Parakou Shipping Pte Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: The company entered creditors' voluntary liquidation following a collapse in the freight market and a significant contingent liability from a charterparty dispute.
The company sold its fleet (OPL Vessels) to a related entity and authorized US$3.1m in bonuses to directors while in the 'vicinity of insolvency'.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether transactions involving the sale of vessels and the payment of bonuses to directors were executed in breach of fiduciary duties to creditors or constituted transactions at an undervalue.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors of a company in the vicinity of insolvency must prioritize the interests of creditors; transactions at an undervalue are voidable if the consideration is significantly lower than market value.
Party_Details.Application: The liquidator sought to claw back funds transferred to related parties, arguing the company was insolvent and the assets were stripped to defraud creditors.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal found the company was insolvent at the relevant time. While it reversed the finding on the vessel sale (applying a fleet discount), it upheld the liability for bonuses and debt repayments as these prejudiced creditors.

Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JA, Steven Chong JA
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Liu Cheng Chan & Chik Sau Kam

Facts.Text: As directors of the company, the individuals authorized substantial bonus payments and salary increases to themselves while the company faced mounting charterparty claims.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether directors breach their fiduciary duties by authorizing personal bonuses when a company is insolvent or near-insolvent.
Party_Details.Rule: A director's duty to act in the best interests of the company shifts to the creditors once the company is in a state of insolvency or near-insolvency.
Party_Details.Application: The directors argued the payments were commercially justified rewards for past service, but the court evaluated the timing against the company's deteriorating financial state.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held the directors liable for the bonuses and salary increases, as these payments were not in the interests of the company's creditors during the vicinity of insolvency.

Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JA, Steven Chong JA
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Parakou Investment Holdings Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: The entity received S$9,812,543 in debt repayments from the insolvent company and purchased the OPL Vessels at a price the Liquidator alleged was an undervalue.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a related corporate entity is liable for knowing receipt or dishonest assistance regarding preferential debt repayments and asset transfers.
Party_Details.Rule: Liability arises if the entity participates in a breach of fiduciary duty with the requisite knowledge of the underlying breach.
Party_Details.Application: The entity benefited from debt repayments that bypassed other creditors; the court examined whether the vessel purchase price reflected a fair market value.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The entity was held liable for the debt repayments (PIH Repayments) via knowing receipt. However, it was not liable for the vessel sale as the court accepted a 'fleet discount' as a valid commercial adjustment.
